# Research: text-hierarchy-sim-ui

*Auto-generated by AIDLC planning session*

**Question:** Typography scales, weights, and spacing used by modern sim UIs to avoid the 'debug overlay with brown borders' feeling. Concrete size ramps for primary action, objective, status, and ambient text at 1080p/1440p.

---

# Text Hierarchy for Sim UI: Typography Scales, Weights, and Spacing

## Why sim UIs read as "debug overlay with brown borders"

Before prescribing a scale, it is worth naming the failure pattern directly. The "debug overlay" feeling comes from a small, consistent set of typography mistakes:

1. **One weight, one size.** Every label renders at 14px Regular. Hierarchy collapses; the eye has no entry point.
2. **Monospaced numerics with proportional labels.** Cash values jitter while labels breathe — looks like a stats dump, not a product.
3. **No optical size curve.** The same font is used at 11px and 32px. Small sizes get muddy; large sizes look anemic.
4. **Default line-height (1.0–1.2).** Paragraphs and data rows collide. The UI feels crammed.
5. **Zero letter-spacing discipline.** ALL CAPS headers at 0 tracking look smashed; body copy at +20 tracking looks stretched.
6. **Brown/beige panel fills + black text + no surface elevation.** Everything floats at the same z-depth. RollerCoaster Tycoon got away with this because the pixel art carried the tone; at 1080p vector UI, it reads as unfinished.
7. **Identical text styling across roles.** Objective, tooltip, HUD stat, menu item, and error all use the same atom. A sim has ~6 distinct text roles and needs ~6 visually distinct atoms.

The fix is not "more polish" — it is a **named, enforced text role system** with a deliberate size ramp, two or three weights, tracking adjustments per size, and spacing that scales with the role.

## The six text roles in a sim UI

Every string in a sim HUD belongs to exactly one of these roles. If you cannot name the role, the string should not ship.

| Role | Purpose | Example strings |
|---|---|---|
| **Display** | The one moment that needs to feel like a title card. | Day-summary header ("Day 14 — Black Friday"), store-name drawer header |
| **Primary action** | Buttons and confirm affordances the player clicks to advance. | "Open Store," "Close Day," "Confirm Price" |
| **Objective / milestone** | Current goal or step the player should read once and remember. | "Earn $2,400 to unlock Pocket Creatures," "Grade 3 more cards" |
| **Status readout** | Live, frequently-changing numbers. | Cash on hand, foot traffic, day/time, reputation |
| **Body / interface** | Menu items, list labels, form fields, tooltip body. | "Haggle," "Restock," "Refurbishment: Clean / Repair / Restore" |
| **Ambient / meta** | Flavor, timestamps, footnotes, debug-like metadata the player ignores most of the time. | "Tue · 3:14pm," "autosaved," tier tag on a card |

Six roles. Not twelve. If you need a seventh, collapse two existing ones.

## Recommended typeface stack

Avoid the "one font does everything" trap. Two families is the sweet spot.

- **UI sans (primary):** A geometric-humanist sans with real optical sizes and a tabular numeric set. Good candidates: **Inter** (free, ships tabular figures and an optical "display" cut), **IBM Plex Sans**, **Manrope**, **Outfit**. For a 2000s mall feel with modern readability, Inter set slightly tighter works well.
- **Numeric (status readouts only):** The **tabular-figures** variant of the UI sans. Do not introduce a separate monospaced family — it reads as "debug." You want tabular **proportional-looking** digits, not Courier.
- **Accent (display role only, optional):** A single display face for store-identity moments (store drawer headers, day-summary title). Keep it restricted; using it anywhere else dilutes the effect.

Do **not** use: default Godot font, Arial, Helvetica at small sizes, anything described as "retro pixel" unless every single element in your UI is pixel art (it isn't — you're at 1080p vector).

## The size ramp

Two targets: 1080p (the default viewport) and 1440p (the "looks right on my good monitor" case). The ramp uses a ~1.25 modular scale (major third) with role-driven deviations. All values are in **logical px**, which Godot maps to screen px via the viewport's content scale.

### 1080p baseline

| Role | Size (px) | Weight | Line-height | Letter-spacing | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Display | **32** | 700 | 1.15 | -0.01em | Optical "display" cut if available; tracking tightens at size |
| Primary action | **18** | 600 | 1.20 | 0 | Button label; never below 16px |
| Objective headline | **20** | 600 | 1.25 | 0 | The "current goal" line on the objective rail |
| Objective body | **14** | 400 | 1.45 | +0.005em | Supporting detail under headline |
| Status label | **11** | 600 | 1.20 | +0.06em | ALL CAPS, high tracking, e.g. "CASH" |
| Status value | **22** | 600 | 1.15 | -0.005em | Tabular figures, e.g. "$2,418" |
| Body / interface | **14** | 400 | 1.45 | 0 | Menu rows, tooltip body |
| Body / interface (strong) | **14** | 600 | 1.45 | 0 | Emphasis inside body copy, not a separate size |
| Ambient / meta | **12** | 400 | 1.30 | +0.01em | Timestamps, footnotes, card tier tags |

### 1440p (and up)

Scale by ~1.25, then round to even pixels and re-check at 11/12 because small sizes do not scale linearly without hinting damage.

| Role | 1080p | 1440p |
|---|---|---|
| Display | 32 | 40 |
| Primary action | 18 | 22 |
| Objective headline | 20 | 26 |
| Objective body | 14 | 16 |
| Status label | 11 | 12 (do **not** round up to 14; small-caps look requires the size delta from the value) |
| Status value | 22 | 28 |
| Body / interface | 14 | 16 |
| Ambient / meta | 12 | 13 |

For 4K, do not scale again — raise the viewport content-scale factor and let the 1440p ramp do its work. Pushing the display role past ~44px starts to look like a PowerPoint title.

## Weight discipline

Use **three weights only: 400, 600, 700**. No 500. No 800. Not because 500 and 800 are bad, but because every added weight is a decision the UI has to defend at every screen, and sim UIs are dense enough that weight should mean *role*, not *mood*.

- **400 Regular** — body, objective body, ambient.
- **600 Semibold** — status label, status value, objective headline, primary action, body-strong.
- **700 Bold** — display only.

If you ever reach for italic: don't. In UI sans at 12–14px, italic just reads as "lightly damaged." Use weight or color for emphasis.

## Letter-spacing and the ALL CAPS rule

Two rules catch most of the debug-overlay feel:

1. **ALL CAPS text below 14px needs +50 to +80 tracking** (in em units: +0.05em to +0.08em). "CASH" at 11px with 0 tracking looks smashed; at +0.06em it becomes a label.
2. **Display sizes above 28px need negative tracking** (−0.01em to −0.02em). Large default tracking makes titles look like airport signage.

Everything between those extremes (14–24px body and status) sits at or near 0.

## Line-height rules

- **Display:** 1.10–1.15. Tight.
- **Headline / objective / primary action:** 1.20–1.25.
- **Body paragraphs and tooltip bodies:** 1.40–1.50. This is the single biggest lever against the "crammed" feeling. Godot defaults often come in under 1.2 — fix this at the theme level, not per-label.
- **Status value (single-line):** 1.10–1.15. You want the number to feel like a digit on a register, not a paragraph.
- **Ambient / footer rows:** 1.25–1.30.

## Spacing: the 4/8 grid, role-aware

Spacing is the other half of hierarchy. Pick a **4px base unit** and step in 4s up to 16, then 8s up to 48. Concretely:

`4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48`

Avoid 2, 5, 6, 10, 14 — these are where hand-tuned layouts come from and why nothing lines up later.

### Role-to-spacing defaults

| Relationship | Space |
|---|---|
| Between a label and its value (status pair) | **4px** |
| Between sibling rows in a list | **8px** |
| Between an objective headline and its body | **4px** |
| Between an objective card and the next | **16px** |
| Between a section header and its first row | **12px** |
| Between drawer sections | **24px** |
| Button internal padding (horizontal) | **16px** |
| Button internal padding (vertical) | **10px** (→ 44px min touch height with 18px label + 1.2 line-height) |
| Panel edge padding | **16px** at 1080p, **20px** at 1440p |
| Between the HUD edge and the viewport edge | **16px** minimum; **24px** is safer on 16:9 |

### The "label sits on the value" pattern

For status readouts, do not stack them with generous spacing — that reads as a dashboard. Instead:

```
CASH                    <- 11/600/+0.06em, color: --text-muted
$2,418.50               <- 22/600/-0.005em, tabular, color: --text-primary
```

4px between the two. 16px between this pair and the next status pair. The tight internal spacing is what signals "these two strings are one thing."

## Color and contrast (because typography fails without it)

You cannot fix the brown-border feeling with sizes alone. Three token tiers are enough:

- `--text-primary` — #F4F1EA (on dark surfaces) or #1A1714 (on light). For the status value, display, primary action.
- `--text-secondary` — 70% luminance of primary. For body, objective body.
- `--text-muted` — 50% luminance of primary. For ambient, status labels, timestamps.

Contrast floor: **WCAG AA** (4.5:1 for body, 3:1 for 18px+/600). At 11px the status label must still clear 4.5:1 even though it is "just a label" — muted does not mean low-contrast.

Never use saturated brown for borders. If you need a warm 2000s-mall feel, push the *surface* color warm (#2A2520, #F4EEE3) and keep text color neutral. Warm text on warm surface is the debug-overlay smell.

## Tabular figures: the one thing most sim UIs miss

Every numeric readout that can change — cash, time, foot traffic, reputation, price, quantity, timer — must use the font's **tabular-figures** OpenType feature (`tnum`). Otherwise the digits shift width as they change and the HUD twitches every tick. In Godot, this is set on the theme's font variation; Inter, Plex, and Manrope all ship tnum.

Rule: if the string contains a digit that updates at runtime, it gets tnum. If it is a static label like "Day 1 tutorial," it does not need tnum (and looks slightly better without it).

## Concrete worked example: the Mallcore HUD strip

Top-of-screen HUD with four status pairs, one objective, and one primary action.

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DAY         CASH           TRAFFIC      REP            [OBJECTIVE]│
│  14          $2,418.50      127 /hr      ★★★☆           Earn $2,400│
│  Tue·3:14p                                              to unlock  │
│                                                         Pocket Crtr│
│                                         [ OPEN STORE ]             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- `DAY / CASH / TRAFFIC / REP`: 11/600/+0.06em, `--text-muted`.
- `14 / $2,418.50 / 127 /hr / ★★★☆`: 22/600/-0.005em, tnum, `--text-primary`.
- `Tue·3:14p`: 12/400/+0.01em, `--text-muted`, 4px below "14."
- `[OBJECTIVE]` label: 11/600/+0.06em, `--text-muted`.
- `Earn $2,400 to unlock Pocket Creatures`: 20/600/1.25, `--text-primary`, 4px below label, max 3 lines.
- `[ OPEN STORE ]` button label: 18/600/0, internal padding 16x10, surface `--accent`, text `--surface`.
- 24px between each status group. 32px between the last status and the objective block. 16px panel edge padding.

This is nine text atoms on one strip using three sizes for the value tier (22, 20, 18), one shared muted label size (11), and one ambient size (12). Three weights total (400, 600). That's the whole ramp doing the work.

## Anti-patterns to actively refuse

- Beveled panel borders with 2px highlight / 2px shadow in warm brown. This is the single strongest "2005 shareware" signal. Use flat surfaces with a 1px inner stroke at 10% opacity, or a soft drop shadow.
- Drop shadows on text at sizes below 20px. Blurs the letterform; no readability gain.
- Underlined labels to indicate interactivity. Use hover/focus state tokens.
- Icons at 16px next to 14px body text — the icon visually outweighs the text. Match icon size to **cap height**, not font size. For 14px text, that usually means a 12px or 13px icon optical-centered on the baseline.
- Mixing font weights *within* a single data value ("$**2,418**.50" with the cents lighter). Looks clever in a mock, jitters in motion.
- Using italics for tier names ("*Mint*," "*Near Mint*"). Use a small tag component with weight 600 and a tier color instead.
- Status labels in sentence case ("Cash"). Sentence case at small sizes blurs with body text. ALL CAPS + tracking is the contract that says "this is metadata, not content."

## Implementation checklist (Godot 4.6)

1. Theme declares one `FontVariation` for UI sans with `tnum` enabled, a second without `tnum` for body/ambient.
2. Each role becomes a named theme type: `DisplayLabel`, `PrimaryActionLabel`, `ObjectiveHeadline`, `ObjectiveBody`, `StatusLabel`, `StatusValue`, `BodyLabel`, `AmbientLabel`. Do not style labels inline.
3. Three color tokens: `text_primary`, `text_secondary`, `text_muted`. All text pulls from these.
4. One spacing constants file: `SPACING_4, SPACING_8, SPACING_12, SPACING_16, SPACING_24, SPACING_32, SPACING_48`. No magic numbers in `.tscn`.
5. Content-scale stretch mode: `canvas_items`, stretch aspect `expand`, viewport 1920×1080 base. The 1440p ramp is a **second theme variant** toggled at runtime, not per-label scaling.
6. A debug overlay screen that renders every role at both scales side by side. Check it after any theme change. If a role disappears into another role, the ramp is broken.

## Summary: one ramp, three weights, two scales

- **Roles, not labels.** Six named text roles; every string has exactly one role.
- **Ramp at 1080p:** 32 / 22 / 20 / 18 / 14 / 12 / 11, weights 700/600/400.
- **Ramp at 1440p:** 40 / 28 / 26 / 22 / 16 / 13 / 12, same weights.
- **Tracking:** +0.06em for 11px ALL CAPS labels, −0.01em for 32px+ display, 0 elsewhere.
- **Line-height:** 1.15 display, 1.20–1.25 headlines/buttons, 1.45 body, 1.30 ambient.
- **Spacing:** 4/8 grid; 4px inside a pair, 16–24px between groups, 16–20px panel padding.
- **Numerics:** always tabular, never monospaced.
- **Color:** three text tiers, WCAG AA floor, warm surfaces only — never warm text on warm fills.

Apply this ramp consistently and the "debug overlay with brown borders" problem dissolves without adding any art. The sim reads as a product because its type system behaves like one.
